title: Eastern Europe’s uneven front: How the East Flank nations stand on Ukraine
content:
Russia’s full-scale invasion of Ukraine in 2022 upended the security balance of Europe and forced its eastern neighbors to confront hard questions about their own safety, alliances, and political identity.

The policies of Eastern Flank countries toward Ukraine reveal a complex landscape characterized by both solidarity and divergence. This report examines the positions of nine countries across NATO and the EU’s eastern flank – Bulgaria, Czechia, Finland, Hungary, Latvia, Lithuania, Romania, and Slovakia – toward Ukraine. All these countries share a border of anxiety and a history marked by Moscow’s shadow. But their responses to the war have not been the same. Some, like Poland and the Baltic states, treat Ukraine’s survival as inseparable from their own. Others, notably Hungary and Slovakia, have balked at sending weapons and adopted a cautious, even contrarian tone.

Military and non-military support: from strategic commitment to cautious assistance

The frontline supporters

Poland, Lithuania, Latv

creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: Orbán, Magyar, and an Army of Bots. The Most Toxic Campaign in Hungary’s History
content:
The Hungarian election campaign has become a testing ground for AI and Russian disinformation. Just days before the most important vote in years, the country is gripped by an informational chaos that completely blurs the line between truth and falsehood.

The approaching April 12, 2026, is more to Hungarians than just another date on the electoral calendar. It is a moment when the country’s fate hangs in the balance, and for Viktor Orbán and his Fidesz party – the toughest test since taking power in 2010. A powerful rival has appeared on the horizon: Péter Magyar and his Tisza party. However, what was supposed to be a celebration of democracy has turned into a technological and disinformation nightmare.

Hungarian voters find themselves right in the middle of unprecedented chaos. They are bombarded from all sides by AI-generated fake tapes, espionage accusations, and massive attacks by Russian troll farms. Truth has become a highly scarce commodity in Hungary, and the election campaign has long since abandoned the rules of fair play.

Ukraine

creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: Produce Less, Earn More: EU Debates Solutions to Milk Crisis
content:
Slovakia calls on the EU to pay farmers to produce less milk, but the European Commission has so far refused. 

Slovakia has officially requested in a letter that the European Commission provide financial assistance to dairy cattle farmers, who are facing increasing losses due to falling milk prices.

The appeal was also signed by Bulgaria, Hungary, and Italy. The letter was sent to the Commission ahead of last week’s EU Agriculture and Fisheries Council (AGRIFISH), where Slovak Agriculture Minister Richard Takáč (Smer-SD) also spoke about the critical situation.

“Our dairy farmers are clearly facing a crisis. I honestly do not understand why we are allowing this sector to become exhausted when we have tools to address the crisis,” he stated before the other agriculture ministers.

In its appeal, Slovakia warns that milk production at current prices and with rising energy and other production input costs is unprofitable and unsustainable in the long term.

According to the

title: Once “on the side of the angels” and condemning the invasion of Georgia: how Viktor Orbán’s policy changed after meeting Putin
content:
He can be regarded as dictator Vladimir Putin’s most valuable asset in Europe – Hungary’s Prime Minister Viktor Orbán is not only attempting to undermine the European Union’s (EU) unity regarding Russia’s invasion of Ukraine, but also to slow down aid to Kyiv.

In the media, the Hungarian leader has even been described as one of the most significant “useful idiots” for Moscow, although he may well have his own calculations and conviction in the correctness of his policy.

“Among all of Russia’s useful idiots, few have tried to make themselves more useful than the Hungarian Prime Minister,” Foreign Policy magazine wrote in 2024. The question is: how did it all begin, and why? Fairly precise answers to the first part can be found more than ten years in the past, but the second remains less clear – leaving observations and speculation.

Returning to 2026, as parliamentary elections in Hungary approach, in which Viktor Orbán is expected to face unprecedented competition, the official pro-Russian stance of Budapest, along with its attitude towards Ukraine and Brussels, holds a highly visible place in the campaign
snippet: He can be regarded as dictator Vladimir Putin’s most valuable asset in Europe – Hungary’s Prime Minister Viktor Orbán is not only attempting to undermine the European Union’s (EU) unity regarding Russ
creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: Does Latvia really have no system for tracking immigrants?
content:
March in Latvia has been a month full of various alarming events: many Riga residents and social media users were riled up by mass Muslim prayers on a street in Pļavnieki, prompting horror at what was portrayed as an uncontrolled invasion of Muslims.

Many users on X shared a 2022 news item in Russian about an agreement that would allow workers from Uzbekistan to enter Latvia, though this also drew a sharp backlash pointing out that the story was outdated.

Although the media outlet from which the story was taken is neither visible nor identified in the screenshot circulated on X, Delfi found a publication with that headline, publication time, and cover image – credited to Uzbekistan’s Foreign Ministry and showing then Latvian Foreign Minister Edgars Rinkēvičs and Uzbekistan’s Foreign Minister Abdulaziz Kamilov – on the website of the news agency Красная весна (Red Spring), which still offers news from Russia.

Overall such narative strenghtens myth that migration in Latvia is untracked and out of control. In a tense security environment on Europe’s eastern flank,

creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: Is the EU planning to introduce US ICE-style methods to combat illegal migration?
content:
Claims have been circulating on social media and in several foreign media outlets in recent weeks that the European Union is developing regulations that would allow the introduction of methods similar to those used by the U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) agency.

The ICE methods have sparked outrage in many parts of the world – including raids on homes and educational institutions, extensive police checks, racial profiling, document confiscation, and sending migrants to deportation centers in third countries.

However, an analysis of information provided by European Union institutions and the content of the draft legislation shows that these claims are significantly exaggerated. Latvia’s Ministry of the Interior also emphasizes that there is no basis for the claim that the EU is planning to introduce ICE practices.

New regulation – yes, “ICE model” – no

Back in March 2025, the European Commission (EC) came forward with a proposal for new regulations to replace the existing Return Directive, which has been in force since 2008. The aim of the new regulation i
snippet: Claims have been circulating on social media and in several foreign media outlets in recent weeks that the European Union is developing regulations that would allow the introduction of methods similar
creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: SAFE Explained: Everything You Need to Know
content:
A loan our grandchildren will repay, or a unique opportunity to rapidly rearm military? We unpack the doubts surrounding the EU’s SAFE programme.

The SAFE initiative is widely seen as one of the biggest successes of Poland’s presidency of the Council of the European Union. It was during Poland’s six-month term at the helm of the Council that the regulation establishing this mechanism was adopted.

“This is an unprecedented instrument that will strengthen our defence capabilities and support our defence industry. The more we invest in our security and defence, the better we deter those who seek to harm us,” said then Polish Minister for European Affairs, Adam Szłapka.

With the European Commission launching the SAFE instrument to bolster EU defence, a growing number of concerns and misleading claims is emerging around it. The most frequently repeated arguments and clarify the key issues are addressed below.

What is SAFE and how does it work?

The Security and Action for Europe instrument (SAFE) is a new EU financial mechanism designed to support m

creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: “Completely Irresponsible”. Czech Deputy Minister Slammed for Sharing Fake Photo
content:
A Czech deputy minister shared a manipulated image suggesting a link between activist Mikuláš Minář and a person accused of an arson attack. According to researcher Veronika Víchová, the case reflects a broader disinformation tactic aimed not at proving anything, but at creating confusion.

Within the span of two days in March, two major events unfolded in Czechia.

On 20 March, a warehouse belonging to LPP Holding in Pardubice caught fire. A previously unknown pro-Palestinian group calling itself the Earthquake Faction claimed responsibility the same day, alleging the company had supported Israel by supplying drones.

The following day, an estimated 250,000 people gathered at Prague’s Letná Plain to protest against government policies that, according to organisers, were undermining democracy, media freedom and the rule of law.

The demonstration was organised by the civic movement Million Moments for Democracy, founded in 2018 and known for staging large-scale anti-government protests, including a major rally at the same location in 2019.

A Manipulated Image

Both

creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: How a myth spread: Slovakia is not blocking Ukraine loan or 20th Russia sanctions package
content:
Slovakia is widely portrayed in domestic and foreign media as blocking two major EU decisions related to Ukraine, but these claims stem from a misunderstanding of the situation. We explain how these interpretations emerged and what the facts actually are. 

The unclear positions of Robert Fico’s coalition have repeatedly caused confusion. This time, it has spread into both Slovak and international media.

Most of them incorrectly report that Slovakia, together with Viktor Orbán, is blocking a €90 billion loan to Ukraine, or that the two countries jointly blocked the 20th package of anti-Russian sanctions in February – and continue to do so.

Both of these claims – meaning unintentionally spread falsehoods – have a somewhat understandable basis. 

They stem mainly from chaotic statements by Slovak government officials or from a misunderstanding of the complex processes within EU institutions. The euBrief portal therefore offers an explanation of how things actually stand.

Fact: Only Hungary is blocking the loan to Ukraine<

creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: The Sidelined Superpower: How the EU Lost Its Grip on Iran
content:
A decade ago, Brussels was the linchpin of the Iran nuclear deal. Today, as the US and Israel edge toward military action, the EU watches from the sidelines, its diplomatic clout all but evaporated.

EU foreign policy was formalised with the creation of the High Representative — a role meant to bridge the European Commission and the European External Action Service. In theory, this “structuring” was supposed to strengthen coherence. In practice, it exposed a core weakness: limited political authority.

Foreign leaders consistently preferred to deal directly with national governments rather than with a supranational diplomat lacking direct democratic legitimacy. From the outset, this undermined the EU’s ability to act decisively on the global stage.

And yet, despite these structural constraints, there were moments when EU diplomacy proved its value.

The Iran deal: Europe at its peak

The clearest example was the Joint Comprehensive Plan of Action (JCPOA). Negotiated over 20 months between 2013 and 2015, the agreement brought together the United States, China, Russia, Germany, the
snippet: A decade ago, Brussels was the linchpin of the Iran nuclear deal. Today, as the US and Israel edge toward military action, the EU watches from the sidelines, its diplomatic clout all but evaporated. E
